and interest income on the cash pile, not durable operating earnings. The "ROE 37%" is a mirage. Operating
activities used roughly $9.1 million of cash in 2025, and the company is now exposed to a third-party credit risk
concentration in the Scilex Notes — Scilex itself disclosed substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a
going concern, which would impair recoverability of the principal and severely dent book value. Capital
allocation has also drifted from drug development into private real estate and equity stakes, which is a classic
late-stage biotech "zombie" pattern. Management is also paying special dividends out of capital, accelerating the
wind-down of the war chest.
The bull case is straightforward arithmetic: at roughly $50M market cap with ~$46M of cash plus carrying value
of investments and the Scilex Notes, the operating biotech and the POD platform are essentially free. If the
planned 60-patient bridging study in 2H 2026 reads out positively, oral insulin gets a second life with an
institutional partner. The OraTech POD spin-out could surface hidden platform value if any oral-peptide partner
(GLP-1 analogues being the obvious target) takes a license. Insider ownership remains elevated, the share count is
small, and any positive catalyst into a $50M shell with $46M cash would re-rate sharply. Optionality, not earnings
whatScreenCantSee:
The reported earnings are dominated by realized and unrealized investment gains plus interest on the cash and
Scilex Notes — not drug royalties or licensing revenue. The 10-K explicitly flags that Scilex has disclosed
substantial doubt about going concern, meaning a meaningful chunk of the "net cash" line item is actually senior
secured paper of a distressed Nasdaq pharma. The OraTech share-purchase transaction shifts the POD oral
peptide platform out of the parent, so even a successful 2H 2026 pilot would economically benefit OraTech, with
Oramed retaining only contractual milestones. The Lifeward exit (medical robotics) and Israel real estate JV are
unrelated diversifications that complicate the "biotech" label entirely.

name: SITE Centers Corp.
analystSummary:
SITE Centers is the residual real estate vehicle left over after the October 1, 2024 spin-off of Curbline Properties
(CURB), which received the company's 79 convenience retail properties — the highest-quality, lowest-cap-rate
slice of the portfolio. What remains at SITC is a thin and shrinking collection of larger open-air shopping centers,
predominantly community/power-center format, in active disposition mode. The 10-K describes the entity as
essentially executing a wholly-owned wind-down: management is "currently in various stages of marketing
several wholly-owned assets," and the company expressly references the "anticipated winding up of its
operations." Aggregate occupancy was 85.9% on a pro rata basis at 12/31/2025, down from 90.6% the year prior
— a meaningful step-down that reflects both portfolio mix and tenant attrition.
The bear case is that the 1.6x P/E and 0.85x P/B are accounting artifacts, not fundamental value signals. Earnings
have been inflated by gains on disposition; book value will continue to be eroded by any disposition prices below
carrying value, plus continued G&A; drag during the wind-down. The remaining centers are the harder-to-sell,
lower-quality power-center boxes that institutional buyers are walking away from. The Mortgage Facility imposes
ongoing financing friction, and the company has paid out substantial special dividends ($227.7 million increase in
dividends in 2025), shrinking the equity base further. There is real risk that the residual NAV falls short of the
current market quote once dispositions clear at realized prices, particularly if cap rates expand for off-mall power
centers.
The bull case is a classic liquidation arbitrage: the market is mis-pricing a vehicle whose stated business is to
monetize its remaining assets and return capital. If the disposition program clears at or near carrying value, the
stub equity should distribute meaningfully more in special dividends than the current market cap implies. The
10-K confirms management is actively marketing assets and the Mortgage Facility provided liquidity to retire
senior unsecured notes, simplifying the capital structure. Class A Preferred shares were redeemed for $175.0
million in 2024, which removes a senior claim. For a patient distressed-arb investor with a 12-24 month horizon
whatScreenCantSee:
The post-spin SITC is structurally not a going-concern REIT — the 10-K openly references the "anticipated
winding up of its operations," which most data providers do not flag. The remaining unencumbered asset pool is
shrinking (the Mortgage Facility encumbers many of the better assets), so timing of dispositions matters more
than reported FFO. The Shared Services Agreement with Curbline means a meaningful portion of the cost base is
contractually tied to a separate company's operating tempo. Combined GLA, occupancy step-down to 85.9%, and
ABR of $22.61/sf are below peer open-air averages, confirming the lower-quality residual. Dividend cadence
going forward should be lumpy special distributions, not a steady REIT yield.

name: Black Diamond Therapeutics, Inc.
analystSummary:
Black Diamond is a precision oncology platform whose entire enterprise value now sits on one molecule:
silevertinib (formerly BDTX-1535), a fourth-generation oral mutant-EGFR inhibitor being developed in two
distinct lung-cancer settings — non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and EGFR-altered glioblastoma (GBM). In
March 2025 the company struck a partnership with Servier (a private French oncology company) that brought
capital and validation onto the platform. The 10-K reports the company had cash, cash equivalents and
investments of $128 million at year-end 2025, against an accumulated deficit of $464 million and a 2025 net loss
of $69.7 million, with cash provided by operating activities of $29.6 million for the year (driven by the Servier
upfront, not by product sales). The reported "net income" line is partnership-driven and does not represent
recurring earnings power.
The bear case is that single-asset, single-target oncology biotechs trade at razor-thin margins of safety. The
reported P/E of 7.3 is meaningless — it reflects one-time deal income. EGFR exon-20 and atypical-EGFR
NSCLC is a competitive arena (Takeda's Exkivity was withdrawn; Johnson & Johnson's Rybrevant + Lazcluze
and Dizal's sunvozertinib all compete). GBM enrollment is notoriously slow and expensive, with high failure
rates. Cash runway depends on burn pace post-Servier; with operating losses of $69.7M and roughly $128M of
cash, a non-deal-driven runway is approximately 18-22 months absent further partnerships or financings. A
negative Phase 2 readout, partner walkaway, or competitive data readout from an established EGFR program
could compress the equity by 50-70% within a single trading session.
The bull case rests on silevertinib's differentiated profile: fourth-generation EGFR inhibitors target both classical
EGFR mutations and the family of "atypical" mutations that escape osimertinib, plus brain penetrance which
matters for both NSCLC CNS metastases and primary GBM. If the GBM Phase 2 produces a clinically
meaningful response rate in newly-diagnosed EGFR-altered glioblastoma — a setting with no approved targeted
therapy and median survival under 18 months — the molecule could earn breakthrough designation and a
whatScreenCantSee:
The 10-K's revenue and net-income lines are dominated by Servier collaboration recognition (upfront and
milestones), not recurring product cash flow — investors who anchor on the screen-reported P/E of 7.3 are
mis-pricing the asset. The accumulated deficit of $464 million highlights the cash-incineration history typical of
platform biotechs. Both pivotal indications (NSCLC and GBM) are running concurrently, doubling enrollment
burden and execution risk. Patient-enrollment difficulty in EGFR-altered GBM is explicitly flagged as a
forward-looking risk in the filing. The shelf registration and at-the-market equity program are live, meaning
dilution at any positive datapoint is the base case for funding pivotal work.

name: Kingstone Companies, Inc.
analystSummary:
Kingstone is a small specialty insurance holding company whose operating subsidiary, Kingstone Insurance
Company (KICO), writes personal-lines (predominantly homeowners) and commercial auto coverage primarily in
New York, where it ranked the 12th largest writer of homeowners insurance in 2024. The defining catalyst is the
Change in Market Dynamics: as Allstate, State Farm and other national carriers materially withdrew from the
New York personal-lines market, Kingstone has been one of the primary admitted-market beneficiaries, picking
up replacement policies at significantly improved pricing. The 2025 10-K reports net income of $40.8 million,
compared with $18.4 million in 2024 — a $22.4 million improvement that reflects both rate adequacy and
favorable market share dynamics. On April 14, 2025 KICO entered an agreement to offer replacement quotes to
selected policyholders of a withdrawing competitor under a New York DFS-approved Withdrawal Plan.
The bear case is centered on tail risk and reserve adequacy. KICO's geographic concentration is acute: 98% of
direct written premiums in 2025 came from New York policies (up from 96% in 2024), and Downstate New York
exposes the book to coastal hurricane and noreaster losses that have historically blown up Kingstone's combined
ratio. The reinsurance program (Swiss Re, Hanover Rück, Lancashire, SCOR, others) is high-quality but
expensive, and pricing has hardened across all reinsurance towers in 2024-2025. Reserves on the legacy book of
business — including the run-off of unprofitable policies that drove the 2022-2023 losses — could still develop
adversely. Personal-lines insurance is also a regulated business; rate-increase approvals and regulatory scrutiny of
the post-Withdrawal Plan growth could compress underwriting margins precisely when the reported numbers look
best.
The bull case is a textbook insurance hard-market compounder. Combined ratio has been driven under 100, ROE
is 43% on accounting numbers but trending toward a sustainable mid-teens to low-20s range as the new-business
cohort earns out. The Change in Market Dynamics is a multi-year tailwind: dislocated competitors do not return
overnight to a market they exited, and Kingstone retains the regulatory relationships, agent network, and
whatScreenCantSee:
The 10-K specifically describes the Withdrawal Plan transaction with a competitor, which most equity screens
will not capture as a discrete catalyst — this is regulator-blessed market-share transfer, not organic competition.
The sale of the company's headquarters building to Ulster County (announced February 2025) generates one-time
cash but also signals an asset-light operating model going forward. The reinsurance panel is concentrated among
top-tier global reinsurers, which is positive for credit quality but means renewal pricing flows directly to
underwriting margins. The Cosi Agency multi-state licensing creates an embedded option to extend the model
beyond New York if and when the company chooses to redeploy capital geographically.

name: OMS Energy Technologies Inc.
analystSummary:
OMS Energy Technologies is a Singapore-based oilfield equipment manufacturer providing surface wellheads,
Christmas trees, and specialty connectors and pipes (SC tubular solutions) primarily to upstream operators in the
Middle East (Saudi Arabia is the dominant geography) and the Asia-Pacific region. The 20-F discloses revenue of
$97.5M for fiscal year ended March 31, 2023 (Predecessor) rising to $203 million in the most recent full
successor year ended March 31, 2025, with gross profit reaching the high-$50M range — gross margin in the
high-20s to low-30s. The Saudi Arabian Aramco IKTVA (In-Kingdom Total Value Add) framework — which
incentivizes domestic-content procurement — is a structural tailwind for OMSE's KSA-incorporated subsidiary,
which is positioned as a qualified local supplier for surface production equipment.
The bear case is that OMSE is structurally a small player in an oligopolistic global wellhead market dominated by
Schlumberger (Cameron), Baker Hughes, Halliburton, and NOV. Customer concentration is explicitly flagged as
a material risk: a small number of customers — likely Aramco-affiliated and other NOC-linked operators — drive
a disproportionate share of revenue, and the loss of any one creates a step-function in the income statement.
Backlog is described in the filing as subject to "unexpected adjustments and cancellations." Fixed-price contracts
expose the company to commodity input cost (steel, alloy) inflation. As a recent IPO (the Predecessor/Successor
accounting reflects a 2023 reorganization), OMSE has limited public-market track record, making historical
multiples unreliable. Geopolitical risk (KSA, broader Middle East) and oil-price cyclicality remain ever-present.
The bull case is straightforward cyclical leverage. OMSE benefits from sustained high upstream capex in KSA
driven by Aramco's commitment to maintain production capacity and Jafurah unconventional gas development;
IKTVA provides preferential local-content access; and the Asian platform diversifies the demand base. At 6.5x
earnings, 1.09x book, and net cash equal to 63% of market cap with ROE of 21%, the equity has minimal
balance-sheet risk. If oil prices and rig counts hold, backlog converts cleanly to revenue and gross margin holds in
the high-20s, this is a 1.0-1.5x book story trading at trough cyclical sentiment. The Singapore listing/US ADR
whatScreenCantSee:
The Predecessor/Successor accounting from the June 2023 reorganization (Sumitomo Corporation involvement)
means GAAP-reported historical comparisons are not apples-to-apples — investors keying off LTM multiples
need to reconstruct underlying revenue and margin trajectory. The IKTVA scoring under the Aramco framework
is a meaningful competitive differentiator that screens cannot capture; loss of qualified-local-supplier status
would be devastating. The 20-F flags multiple geographic-concentration and customer-concentration risks but
doesn't disclose top-customer percentages in line; investors should assume Aramco and a small number of NOCs
collectively drive 40%+ of revenue. The wellhead and Christmas tree product line is where OMSE earns its
highest gross margin; SC tubular solutions is more competitive.

name: J.Jill, Inc.
analystSummary:
J.Jill is a 256-store omnichannel women's apparel chain targeting an affluent customer aged 45+, with a heritage
in relaxed, easy-to-wear fits and a roughly 50/50 split between brick-and-mortar and direct (catalog plus
e-commerce). Fiscal 2025 (ended January 31, 2026) revenue was approximately $611M, essentially flat versus
FY24, with reported net income near $39.5M and a net margin of about 6.5%. The company has just under 14.9M
shares outstanding and trades at a single-digit P/E with a 25% reported ROE — figures that imply the market
views the business as a melting catalog asset rather than a durable specialty retailer. Management has raised the
quarterly dividend 12.5% to $0.09 and continued to invest in store openings and the New Hampshire distribution
and contact center.
The bear case is that J.Jill's customer is loyal but old, the catalog channel is structurally declining, and apparel is a
brutally cyclical, fashion-risk business with limited pricing power once promotional pressure resumes. Comps
were essentially flat in FY25; capex jumped 33% to $14.3M, suggesting the easy free-cash-flow harvesting period
of the post-2020 restructuring is ending. The 25% ROE flatters a business whose return on invested capital is
lower once you strip out the depleted equity base and adjust for operating leases. A modest macro shock, a return
to mall traffic decline, or a single bad season of inventory could compress margins by 200-300 bps and turn the
P/E from 6.7x into a value trap.
The bull case is that JILL is a tightly run, niche specialty retailer with a defined customer, a high direct mix that
gives it 1P data, and a balance sheet that has been deleveraged dramatically since the 2020 sale-leaseback
restructuring. At ~$200-300M market cap on roughly $40M of net income, the stock is priced for terminal
decline; any combination of stable comps, modest store-count growth, and continued buyback/dividend capacity
unlocks a re-rating to 9-10x earnings. Tax assets remaining from the restructuring shield cash taxes, boosting
reported FCF conversion. If management continues the dividend hike pattern and adds a buyback, total
shareholder yield could reach 6-8%.
whatScreenCantSee:
The 520,000-sqft New Hampshire distribution and contact center is a single point of failure — it processed 28M
units in FY25 (13M retail, 15M direct) and any disruption (storm, system outage, labor) would hit both channels
simultaneously. The 14.9M share count is unusually small for a public retailer and creates real liquidity friction; a
single index reconstitution or one large holder's exit could move the stock 10%. JILL also still carries a
meaningful deferred tax asset position from the post-COVID restructuring that is not visible in headline P/E.
Watch the gap between reported net income, cash taxes paid, and SBC — operating cash flow is what matters, not
GAAP earnings. Lease liabilities (256 stores) are the real "debt."

name: Finance of America Companies Inc.
analystSummary:
Finance of America is the dominant U.S. reverse mortgage originator and servicer, with roughly 30% HECM
(Home Equity Conversion Mortgage) issuance market share in 2025. The company emerged from a 2024 debt
restructuring and is now profitable: 2025 net income of approximately $103M on revenue of about $497M, a
sharp swing from prior-year losses. Adjusted net income of about $74M was up 429% year over year and adjusted
EBITDA grew 138% to roughly $143M. The Retirement Solutions segment — proprietary and HECM reverse
mortgages plus the emerging Home Equity Investment (HEI) product — is the entire forward thesis. Cash and
equivalents grew to $90M from $47M in 2024. In late 2025 FOA announced an agreement to acquire PHH
Mortgage's reverse mortgage servicing portfolio from Onity, and a $2.5B strategic partnership with Blue Owl that
included a $50M equity investment. Blackstone's residual equity stake was repurchased in February 2026.
The bear case is that this is still a fair-value-accounted balance-sheet business whose reported earnings are
extremely sensitive to interest-rate assumptions, prepayment speeds, and home-price appreciation embedded in
the retained interests on securitized HECMs. A reverse mortgage originator carries massive gross balance-sheet
exposure (HMBS-related obligations are tens of billions) against a tiny equity sliver — book value near $0.54x of
equity reflects that the market does not fully trust the marks. The HEI product (shared appreciation in exchange
for lump-sum cash) is unproven through a downturn; if U.S. home prices roll over, both retained HECM interests
and HEI valuations re-mark down simultaneously. Consumer reverse-mortgage origination is regulator-sensitive
and reputationally fragile.
The bull case is straightforward: post-restructuring, FOA is a now-profitable monopoly-like player in a
structurally growing demographic market (aging boomers with home equity), and Blue Owl's $2.5B commitment
gives it both capital and a credibility stamp. Trading near book at $188M market cap on $103M of GAAP
earnings (P/E ~5.6x, P/B ~0.54), it is priced as if 2024's distress will recur. If reverse-mortgage volumes hold and
HEI scales to meaningful size, the stock should clear book — implying ~85% upside before any earnings
whatScreenCantSee:
GAAP earnings here are not cash. The retained beneficial interests in HMBS securitizations are Level-3 fair-value
assets — the income statement gain on origination is partly an unrealized mark, and discount-rate or
HPA-assumption changes can swing reported earnings sharply quarter-to-quarter. Read the 10-K's sensitivity
tables on discount rates and prepayment assumptions. The Blue Owl partnership terms (warrants, preferred
features, consent rights) materially affect common equity value and warrant close reading. The Blackstone equity
buyback removed a structural overhang but used cash. Finally, HECM origination is dependent on FHA program
continuity — any policy change to the HECM program (loan limits, mortgage insurance premiums,
principal-limit factors) is a binary risk.

name: Yalla Group Limited
analystSummary:
Yalla Group operates the leading voice-chat and casual gaming platforms in the Middle East and North Africa
(MENA) — flagship apps Yalla (a localized voice chat with the cultural "Majlis" room concept), Yalla Ludo (a
Ludo and Domino casual gaming app with integrated voice), and adjacent titles. The numbers are striking: 2025
full-year revenue of approximately $342M, net income of about $148M (43.3% net margin), and Q4 average
MAUs up 8.2% year over year to 44.8M. Cash and equivalents stood at approximately $754.6M at year-end 2025,
dwarfing the roughly $250M market cap. The company executed about $56.6M of buybacks in 2025 and
authorized a new $150M repurchase program in March 2026 over a 24-month window. P/E of 8.4x and P/B of
1.33x understate just how cheap this is on an enterprise basis: at MCap ~$250M minus ~$750M cash, the
operating business is being priced at deeply negative EV — a textbook cash-fortress setup.
The bear case is governance and country risk. Yalla is a Cayman-domiciled ADR with a UAE-based operating
footprint serving MENA users, and there is ongoing investor concern about the verifiability of paying-user
metrics, the strength of revenue auditability, and the historical short-seller report (Swan Street Research, 2022)
that questioned reported numbers — never fully resolved. Paying users actually declined to 10.4M in Q4 versus
growing MAUs, hinting that monetization per user is sliding even as the top of the funnel grows. Cash is large but
if held in jurisdictions with limited capital-controls flexibility, repatriation to ADR holders is uncertain. PCAOB
audit access for MENA-focused issuers, while not a China-style binary, still presents friction.
The bull case is the cleanest in this batch: a profitable, growing, net-cash-rich consumer platform trading below
the value of its cash. Even if you haircut cash 30% for repatriation/governance discount and assume the operating
business is worth zero, you arrive at fair value above the current price. The $150M buyback at current prices
could retire ~50% of the float over two years. Margins of 41% in Q4 imply genuine operating leverage. The
MENA digital consumer market is growing, under-penetrated by Western-platform monetization, and Yalla has
the localized product.
whatScreenCantSee:
Read the 20-F section on cash held by jurisdiction — the historical disclosure shows large balances held at
PRC-headquartered banks, partly in RMB; that is not the same as USD held in Dubai or Singapore. The Cayman
holding/VIE structure here is for MENA-focused operations, not China-VIE risk in the traditional sense, but
management roots and engineering centers have ties to mainland China that influence audit and capital-flow
logistics. The Q4 paying-user decline (PUs down while MAUs up) is the single most important leading indicator
— it suggests ARPU compression or churn at the top of the spending pyramid. Watch related-party transactions
and the share-issuance pattern; a profitable platform with $750M cash that issues ADS to insiders is a red flag any
quarter.

name: Benchmark Bankshares, Inc.
analystSummary:
Benchmark Bankshares is the holding company for Benchmark Community Bank, a 1971-founded Virginia
community bank headquartered in Kenbridge, Virginia, with approximately 18 branches across central Southside
Virginia and northern North Carolina. Fiscal 2025 was a record year: net income of $19.8M ($4.46/share) versus
$15.9M ($3.55) in 2024, a 25.2% increase driven by balance-sheet growth, NIM expansion, and an improved
efficiency ratio. Total assets reached $1.4B (up 11.7%), loans held for investment $1.1B (up 13.5%), deposits
$1.2B (up 11.2%), and shareholders' equity $126.9M (up 15.9%). The annualized dividend is $0.88/share for a
yield of approximately 2.8%, with a payout ratio near 20.5%. Beta is approximately 0.17, reflecting the OTC,
illiquid, deeply-held shareholder base typical of small-town Virginia community banks. P/E ~8.4x, P/B ~1.35x,
ROE ~16%.
The bear case is liquidity and disclosure. BMBN deregistered with the SEC long ago — the latest EDGAR filings
on file are from 2003-2004 — and now reports through state and OTC channels rather than full SEC 10-K
cadence. That makes deep diligence harder for outside investors and creates a permanent valuation discount. Loan
growth of 13.5% in a single year is fast for a community bank and, in a flat-to-down credit cycle, can mask
coming asset-quality issues; with no detailed NPL/charge-off data in mainstream sources, you are buying on faith.
Geographic concentration in rural southern Virginia exposes the bank to a narrow agricultural and small-business
customer base. OTC float means a single seller can move the price 5-10% on any given day.
The bull case is exactly the boring, durable kind of community bank Munger would have admired: 50+ years in
business, recognizable hometown brand, conservative balance sheet, growing book value, paying a meaningful
dividend, and trading at modest multiples. ROE of 16% with a 20% payout ratio implies the retained earnings can
compound book at 12-13% for years if credit holds. At 1.35x book, you're paying a slight premium but receiving
genuine quality and underwriting discipline that has survived multiple cycles. The illiquidity discount is the price
of admission, not a defect.
whatScreenCantSee:
Because BMBN reports primarily through call reports and the OTC Markets disclosure channel rather than SEC
10-K, you should pull the FFIEC Call Report directly for granular detail: NIM, efficiency ratio, NPL ratio,
allowance/loans, Tier 1 capital, CRE concentration, and uninsured-deposit ratio. Local press (Kenbridge Victoria
Dispatch, Farmville Herald) has been the primary disclosure venue for headline earnings. Insider ownership is
concentrated in long-tenured Virginia families; this is functionally a closely-held bank with public-market
liquidity tacked on. Trading volume can be zero on many days. Tangible book per share is roughly $28-29 based
on $126.9M equity / 4.4M shares, but verify against the Call Report.

name: GDEV Inc.
analystSummary:
GDEV (formerly Nexters Global) is a Cyprus-domiciled mobile-game holding company whose primary asset is
the long-running RPG franchise Hero Wars, plus the smaller title Island Hoppers. Hero Wars generated
approximately 93% of 2025 revenue and has surpassed 185M lifetime installs, with the franchise itself having
generated about $1.7B in cumulative revenue. The company emerged from a difficult corporate history: trading
was halted by Nasdaq in February 2022 following Russia's invasion of Ukraine due to Nexters' Russian roots, and
the company subsequently relocated critical personnel from Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus to Cyprus, Kazakhstan,
and Armenia, divesting its Russian subsidiaries to local management. The 2023 reorganization placed GDEV as
the publicly traded holding company. Reported metrics today: P/E ~4.2x, EV/EBITDA ~2.3x, with net cash
representing roughly 39% of market cap — multiples that scream "the market expects this to die."
The bear case is unambiguous: bookings fell 4% in 2024 and another 13% in 2025; Hero Wars Monthly Paying
Users declined 8% in 2024 and 18% in 2025; Q4 2025 revenue of about $90M was down 8% year over year. This
is a single-product mobile game in late franchise life, where the user-acquisition economics across the mobile
gaming sector have deteriorated significantly post-IDFA, and the marginal Hero Wars cohort produces less LTV
than the cost of acquiring it. The 4.2x P/E reflects analysts pricing terminal-value decline. Russian-origin
reputation creates a permanent partner, payments, and platform-relationship overhang — Apple, Google, ad
networks, and institutional investors all impose friction. Regulatory and sanctions risk is not zero even after the
relocation; KYC on remaining cash flows, wallet structures, and historical IP transfers will continue to be
scrutinized.
The bull case requires only that the decline rate slow. Net cash of ~39% of market cap means the operating
business trades at roughly 2.5x EBITDA. If management runs Hero Wars for cash, returns capital aggressively,
and avoids burning balance-sheet on doomed new launches, even a 10-15% annual revenue decline produces an
IRR above 20%. A small $30-50M buyback at current levels could retire 15-25% of float. Optionality on a new
whatScreenCantSee:
Read the 20-F's risk-factor section on sanctions, the divestiture mechanics of the Russian subsidiaries, and the
corporate-residency status of senior management — these are the actual binary risks, not the operating P&L.;
Cash held by jurisdiction matters: balances in Cyprus, Armenia, or Kazakhstan banks have very different
liquidity-to-shareholders profiles than USD held in tier-1 Western banks. Watch UA spend trends quarter to
quarter — if management is still leaning on user acquisition to maintain DAU, the cash will be consumed faster
than headline FCF suggests. Player retention curves for Hero Wars cohorts (1-day, 7-day, 30-day) by year are the
truest measure of franchise health and are buried in management commentary, not screens.

name: Gencor Industries, Inc.
analystSummary:
Gencor Industries is one of the cleanest "cigar-butt-meets-quality" setups in the US small-cap industrial universe.
The Orlando-based company has manufactured heavy equipment for asphalt and highway-construction markets
for over 50 years — counterflow drum plants, batch plants, soil-remediation systems, fluid heat-transfer units, and
combustion controls — selling primarily to North American paving contractors. The screen sees a P/E of 14.2 and
a P/B of 1.00, but those numbers obscure a fortress balance sheet: roughly $136 million in cash and marketable
securities against zero long-term debt at FY2025 year-end, against an enterprise value that is a small fraction of
the equity market cap. Net cash represents an estimated 68% of market cap, which makes the operating business
effectively free at current prices and reframes Gencor as a closed-end investment vehicle wrapped around a
cyclical equipment manufacturer.
The bear case is real and is mostly about capital allocation discipline and cycle timing. Gencor is
family-controlled by the Elliotts, and the company has historically hoarded cash rather than buying back stock
aggressively or instituting a regular dividend. Special dividends are sporadic and unpredictable. The
marketable-securities portfolio injects mark-to-market noise into reported earnings every quarter — the headline
"P/E" moves around with equity markets rather than with the asphalt cycle. More concerning: the FY2025 10-K
disclosed a backlog of $23.6 million versus $56.2 million the prior year, a roughly 58% drop. That signals either
order timing slippage or a genuine near-term contraction in dealer/contractor demand, which would push FY2026
revenues meaningfully lower despite federal IIJA tailwinds in the macro background.
The bull case rests on three pillars. First, the cash pile is genuinely floor-setting — at current prices an investor is
paying near book and inheriting $100m+ of liquid securities. Second, the IIJA highway-funding cycle is still in
mid-deployment through 2027, and Gencor is one of only a few US-domiciled producers (competing with Astec
Industries) of integrated asphalt plants, with a sticky installed-base service revenue stream. Third, family control
creates conditions for either a take-private offer at a premium or a one-time large special dividend if the Elliotts
whatScreenCantSee:
The 10-K's marketable securities footnote is essential: a meaningful portion sits in equity securities, not just
Treasuries, so unrealized gains/losses flow through the P&L; and distort GAAP earnings vs. operating cash
generation. The "real" P/E ex-investments is materially lower than the headline. The backlog disclosure (Dec 1
reporting date convention rather than fiscal year-end) is the single best leading indicator of near-term revenue —
at $23.6m it points to a soft FY2026 first half. Geographic concentration is overwhelmingly US highway/paving
contractors; there is little international diversification. Insider ownership through John Elliott and family is
concentrated enough that minority shareholders are effectively along for whatever capital allocation decision the
Elliotts choose to make next.

name: Comstock Holding Companies, Inc.
analystSummary:
Comstock Holding Companies is a Reston, Virginia-based asset-light real-estate services platform that manages a
portfolio of mixed-use, multifamily, and commercial properties concentrated in the Washington, DC metro
corridor. The reported metrics — P/E 11.2, P/B 2.63, ROE 28% — describe a small, debt-free fee-income
business that has reinvented itself from a builder/developer into a fee-based asset manager. CHCI provides asset
management, property management, leasing, parking (ParkX), and capital markets services to a related-party
anchor portfolio controlled by founder/CEO Christopher Clemente and strategic partner Dwight Schar. Recent
disclosures highlight 2025 revenue growth in the low- to mid-20s percent range, expansion into data-center
adjacent platforms, and continued growth of the recurring-fee base. The thesis is simple: a sub-$200m-mcap
asset-light services company riding the structural tailwind of one of the strongest commercial-real-estate
sub-markets (Northern Virginia / data center alley).
The bear case starts and ends with related-party concentration. CHCI's anchor revenue stream is governed by a
long-term, cost-plus asset management agreement with Comstock Partners, LC ("CP"), an entity controlled by the
same CEO who controls the public company. Effectively, Christopher Clemente sets the fees that he pays himself
as a public-company shareholder. That structure has clear governance risk: any renegotiation, related-party
transfer, or change in CP's underlying property mix can compress public-company economics overnight, and
minority shareholders have limited recourse. The asset-light model also means CHCI doesn't own the underlying
real estate — if CP loses a property, leases up poorly, or sells, the management fee evaporates. Float is small,
ownership is concentrated, and exit liquidity in a stress scenario would be poor.
The bull case is that the related-party structure, while concentrated, has produced consistent fee growth, a
debt-free balance sheet, and high incremental margins because the marginal property added to the platform
requires limited new corporate overhead. Northern Virginia and the broader DC metro continue to absorb
federal-adjacent office demand, multifamily demand from a high-income labor base, and — most importantly —
whatScreenCantSee:
Read Note 13 (related party transactions) carefully — the fee structure, the cost-plus mark-up, and any
amendments to the anchor portfolio agreement matter more than any income-statement line. Also check the
description of the Anchor Portfolio: occupancy, lease maturities, and tenant concentration drive the durability of
the management fee. ParkX is increasingly disclosed as a growth driver and warrants its own segment-level read.
The data-center platform is new and small but is a credible call option. Insider ownership is heavy — Clemente
and Schar between them effectively control the company. There is no analyst coverage of consequence; price
discovery is thin and often news-driven.

name: First Ottawa Bancshares, Inc.
analystSummary:
First Ottawa Bancshares is the Illinois-based holding company for American Commercial Bank & Trust, a
community bank with roots dating to 1865 and a footprint across LaSalle, Grundy, Cook, and surrounding
counties. The bank operates roughly a dozen branches and offers a fairly standard community bank menu:
consumer and commercial deposits, mortgage and consumer lending, commercial and ag loans, trust and fiduciary
services. Headline metrics — P/E 8.5, P/B 1.42, ROE 21% — describe a quietly profitable rural/suburban Illinois
lender trading at a discount to peers, with returns on equity considerably above what most community banks of
similar size achieve. The recurring narrative around FOTB is the combination of decent profitability, deeply
illiquid OTC trading, and an extraordinarily small float that locks out any institutional buyer of size.
The bear case is structural rather than fundamental. FOTB is a true micro-cap community bank — daily volume
measured in hundreds of shares is common — and the holding company recently issued $24.25 million of
subordinated notes, which adds leverage at the holding-company level and pressures consolidated capital ratios
over time. Loan-book concentration to commercial real estate, agriculture, and small-business lending in northern
Illinois is exposed to regional office-market and farm-income cyclicality. The bank's geographic adjacency to
suburban Chicago (Cook and Grundy counties) is a double-edged sword: deposit-rich but credit-exposed. As a
non-NASDAQ-listed name, financial disclosure cadence is less frequent and analyst coverage is essentially zero,
raising adverse-selection risk for outside investors.
The bull case is the classic "fundamental compounder behind an illiquid quote" thesis. A 21% ROE for a
community bank is exceptional — most peers earn 8–11% — and at 1.42x book the implied earnings yield is
double-digit. The 2024 record-profit disclosure suggests the franchise is benefiting from a steeper yield curve and
continued deposit stickiness in its rural Illinois markets. Subordinated debt issuance, while adding leverage, also
funds either organic growth, M&A; optionality, or capital return. For a patient investor able to accumulate shares
slowly and willing to hold for years, FOTB offers the canonical "tiny bank with real earnings power" setup.
whatScreenCantSee:
The most current 10-K available on EDGAR is dated 2012 — FOTB likely deregistered or moved to OTC
reporting only, which means FFIEC Call Reports filed by the bank subsidiary are the cleanest source of financial
truth. Investors should read the latest Call Report for charge-offs, NPL ratios, CRE concentration metrics, and

name: Fidelity D & D Bancorp, Inc.
analystSummary:
Fidelity D & D Bancorp is the Dunmore, Pennsylvania-based holding company for Fidelity Bank, a community
bank operating roughly 21 full-service offices across Lackawanna, Luzerne, Lehigh, and Northampton Counties,
plus a separately branded Fidelity Bank Wealth Management office in Schuylkill County. The franchise is
unusually deposit-rich for its size: total assets reached $2.75 billion in 2025, with loans of $1.89 billion and
deposits of $2.47 billion. The mix matters — a meaningful wealth management and brokerage business rides on
top of the core banking franchise, plus a mortgage center, which give FDBC a more diversified non-interest
income stream than a typical eastern PA community bank. Headline P/E of 9.1 and P/B of 1.06 understate the
underlying quality: 2025 net income of $28.2 million and EPS of $4.86 was up 36% year-over-year, with
non-performing assets at just 0.08% of total assets.
The bear case for FDBC is principally regional and rate-driven. Northeast Pennsylvania is a slow-growth
demographic region; Scranton/Wilkes-Barre population trends are flat to declining, which caps long-term
loan-growth potential without geographic expansion. Branch density at 21 offices for ~$2.75B in assets is on the
higher side and represents real fixed cost. Net interest margin expansion that drove the 36% earnings jump in
2025 was partly a function of rate normalization — if the Fed cuts aggressively in 2026, NIM compression would
reverse some of that benefit. CRE exposure across the loan book — especially small office, hotel, and multifamily
in legacy industrial PA markets — is a credit risk to monitor through the next downturn. Wealth management
AUM is a fee-income lever that depends on equity-market levels.
The bull case is that this is a high-quality franchise that has compounded book value steadily, with an
extraordinary 0.08% NPA ratio that signals disciplined underwriting through multiple cycles. A double-digit ROE
community bank with a well-developed wealth management business trading near tangible book is unusual;
comparable franchises (Univest, ESSA, Penns Woods) typically trade 1.2–1.6x book. The 2025 result
demonstrates operating leverage: revenue growth dropped substantially to the bottom line. Strategic optionality
whatScreenCantSee:
The wealth management segment disclosure is critical — it is structurally higher-multiple than the spread bank
and supports a sum-of-the-parts case for the stock. Read the loan-portfolio breakdown for CRE concentration
ratios versus regulatory guidance (300% of capital threshold). The Schuylkill County wealth office expansion
suggests intent to push the wealth franchise outside the home county, which is the highest-ROE growth lever
available. Insider ownership and proxy voting concentration matter for any future M&A; scenario — the recent
DEF 14A discloses the relevant directors and pay structure. Also note: the bank has historically been conservative
on capital return relative to peers, which is consistent with the long-term hold profile but caps near-term
shareholder yield.

name: BEO Bancorp
analystSummary:
BEO Bancorp is the holding company for Bank of Eastern Oregon, a community bank founded in 1945 by
farmers and ranchers and headquartered in Heppner, Oregon. The bank operates approximately 21 branches plus
three loan production offices spanning 11 eastern Oregon counties, four eastern Washington counties, and one
western Idaho county. Its franchise is distinctive in that it is one of the few remaining independent community
banks specifically focused on agricultural lending in the Pacific Northwest interior — a region underserved by
money-center banks and even most regional banks. Headline numbers — P/E 8.1, P/B 1.35, ROE 18% —
describe an above-average-ROE community bank trading well below the typical 1.5–1.8x book multiple of
profitable Pacific Northwest peers. ICBA recognized BEO among its Top Ag Lenders of 2024, validating the
franchise's positioning.
The bear case mirrors the structural concerns common to all OTC-traded micro-cap community banks. Float is
extraordinarily thin, daily volume is essentially nil for institutional purposes, and price discovery is sporadic.
Concentrated agricultural lending — wheat, cattle, hay, irrigated row crops — is exposed to commodity-price
cycles, drought risk, and federal farm-bill policy. Eastern Oregon's economy is small and slow-growing;
deposit-base growth depends on local economic conditions that have limited demographic tailwinds. Regulatory
burden on small banks continues to scale faster than asset bases, pressuring efficiency ratios. Compliance costs on
a $700m–$800m asset bank are disproportionate, and the bank's wide geographic footprint (21 branches across
three states) implies higher fixed-cost-to-asset ratios than urban peers. Sub-debt or capital raises by similarly sized
peers have been at high coupons in the current rate environment.
The bull case is the inverse of the bear: a sticky agricultural deposit franchise with limited deposit competition, an
18% ROE that few community banks anywhere produce, and a price below 1.4x book. The bank's deep
specialization in ag credit — confirmed by ICBA recognition — is a genuine competitive moat in markets where
money-center underwriters lack expertise. Sticky deposits from multi-generational farm/ranch customers cost less
whatScreenCantSee:
Because BEOB trades on OTC and is not listed on a major exchange, the bank-subsidiary FFIEC Call Report is
the most reliable real-time financial source — it discloses the loan portfolio mix (ag operating, ag real estate,
commercial, consumer), charge-offs, NPL ratios, deposit composition, and capital ratios. Concentration metrics
are critical: any single-borrower or single-commodity concentration above regulatory thresholds is material risk.
Dividend policy has historically been modest and stable, supporting a "bond-like" hold. Insider and director
ownership tends to be high and locally concentrated, which is positive for alignment but reinforces the float
problem. Drought conditions and Pacific Northwest snowpack reports are surprisingly relevant to credit quality
given irrigation dependency.

name: Cal-Maine Foods, Inc.
analystSummary:
Cal-Maine Foods is the largest US shell-egg producer, controlling roughly the largest share of branded and
private-label table eggs through 50 processing/packaging plants and an estimated ~285 million-bird US table-egg
flock backdrop. Fiscal 2025 (ended May 31, 2025) was a banner year: total net sales of $4.3 billion versus $2.3
billion in fiscal 2024, with the net average selling price per dozen at $3.134 (vs $1.932 prior year), a direct
beneficiary of HPAI-driven supply destruction. Management closed the Echo Lake Foods acquisition June 2,
2025, expanding into prepared/value-added foods. The screen flags net-cash, sub-3x EV/EBITDA, and a P/E
around 5x, but those numbers reflect peak-cycle profitability — every long-term investor in CALM has to
underwrite the next downcycle.
The bear case is straightforward: this is a commodity producer trading on cyclical-peak earnings. Conventional
egg prices per dozen of $3.49 in FY25 (vs $1.73 in FY24) won't persist once the depopulated layer flock recovers.
Customer concentration is extreme — Walmart/Sam's alone is 33.6% of net sales, top three customers 49.2% —
and these grocers are already pushing back on pricing. The variable dividend policy (one-third of net income)
means payouts will collapse alongside earnings. Management acquired Echo Lake at peak-cycle cash flow, and as
of April 14, 2025 the founder Adams family ceded "controlled company" status, removing a long-standing
alignment anchor and opening the door to less-disciplined capital allocation.
The bull case rests on structural egg-supply tightness. HPAI-driven culls (over 39 million additional layer hens
depopulated through May 2025) have made supply restoration a multi-year process. Cage-free mandates in CA,
MA, CO, WA and other states force capex that smaller competitors can't absorb, consolidating share toward
CALM. The $500 million share repurchase authorization (Feb 2025) plus 32% net-cash gives optionality to buy
back stock through any trough. Specialty/branded eggs (Egg-Land's Best, Land O' Lakes, 4-Grain) carry better
margins and customer stickiness than commodity. Echo Lake adds a non-shell prepared-foods leg. If you assume
a normalized through-cycle EPS of $4–6 and a 10–12x multiple, fair value lands materially above today's
whatScreenCantSee:
The 36% operating margin in FY25 is a once-in-a-decade outlier driven by HPAI-induced shortage; FY24
operating margin was 13.4% and the prior cyclical trough was negative. The $46.8 million of goodwill is small
(1.5% of total assets) — pre-Echo Lake. Family control just ended April 14, 2025, which is governance-positive
on paper but removes the founder-family discipline that historically constrained M&A.; The Echo Lake purchase
will introduce branded-foods integration risk and cage-free capex acceleration. Cage-free now represents a
growing share of "specialty" volume; specialty pricing of $2.519/dozen actually underperformed conventional in
FY25 because long-term cage-free contracts didn't fully reset. Watch the company's first downcycle response:
will it draw down the new $500M repo authorization aggressively into the trough?

name: Rigel Pharmaceuticals, Inc.
analystSummary:
Rigel is one of the very few sub-$1B commercial-stage biotechs that is actually profitable. The 2025 10-K shows
total revenues of $294.3 million (up from $179.3 million in 2024), driven by $232.0 million in net product sales
and $62.0 million in collaboration revenue. The portfolio is built around three commercialized assets in
hematology/oncology: TAVALISSE (fostamatinib, oral SYK inhibitor for chronic ITP), REZLIDHIA
(olutasidenib, mIDH1 R/R AML), and GAVRETO (pralsetinib, RET-fusion NSCLC and thyroid cancers —
in-licensed from Blueprint in Feb 2024). 2025 product mix: TAVALISSE $158.8M (+51.6% YoY), REZLIDHIA
$31.0M (+34.8% YoY), and the rest from GAVRETO and partner royalties (Kissei in Japan, Grifols in EU). Cash
+ ST investments were $155 million at year-end 2025.
The bear case for RIGL is execution risk on three small drugs against a backdrop of intensifying ITP and AML
competition. TAVALISSE is now seven years post-launch and faces longer-term competition from newer ITP
agents; REZLIDHIA is a niche second-line AML drug for an mIDH1-mutated subset that is being squeezed by
Servier's Tibsovo and other emerging agents. GAVRETO acquired from Roche/Blueprint comes with execution
and supply-chain risk. McKesson alone was 42% of revenue in 2025, an uncomfortable specialty-pharma
concentration. Management explicitly warns recent operating income may not be sustainable. Pipeline depth is
thin; without a fresh in-licensing deal, the long-term growth trajectory flattens, and the biotech-binary nature
returns.
The bull case is that Rigel is doing what the entire sector wishes it could: generating profitable cash flow from a
small commercial book while running a nearly debt-free balance sheet. The 2025 P/E around 1.6x reflects
skepticism that earnings persist, but TAVALISSE 51% growth and REZLIDHIA 35% growth tell a different story
— these drugs are still ramping. Rigel has demonstrated it can integrate in-licensed assets (GAVRETO from
Blueprint, REZLIDHIA from Forma) at attractive economics. Total assets of $513.6 million versus $164.0
million a year earlier reflect the addition of long-term investments. As a small profitable hematology platform
whatScreenCantSee:
The "P/E 1.6" is misleading because 2025 net income includes large one-time items from collaboration revenue
and milestone recognition; the underlying recurring product-driven earnings are lower. Forma deal economics:
Rigel pays tiered royalties on REZLIDHIA net sales from low-teens to mid-thirties percentages — meaning
incremental margin compresses as REZLIDHIA scales. The Kissei (Japan, fostamatinib) and Grifols (EU) royalty
streams are small but high-margin. Customer concentration is severe: McKesson at 42% of total revenue. Cash
$40.6M but short-term investments $114.4M = ~$155M liquidity, which is not the "fortress" some screens might
imply. Pipeline thin — most R&D; spend is on lifecycle extension rather than new molecules.

name: Jiayin Group Inc.
analystSummary:
Jiayin Group is a Shanghai-based online consumer-finance loan-facilitation platform that connects individual
borrowers in China with licensed institutional funding partners and guarantors, earning service fees rather than
balance-sheet interest income. The 20-F shows the typical Chinese ADR Cayman-holdco/VIE structure: investors
own a Cayman shell whose contractual rights to the operating onshore VIE Group entities depend on PRC
contract enforcement. Total net revenue of RMB 5.80 billion in 2024 (down from RMB 5.47 billion in 2023 in
segment terms but disclosed segment data shows volatile comparatives across the consolidating eliminations),
with international expansion underway in Indonesia, Mexico (via EPD investment), and Nigeria. Reported P/E
around 1x and P/B 0.37 reflect the market's aggressive discounting of accounting earnings that carry political-risk
attached.
The bear case is structural and profound: this is a Chinese consumer-lending intermediary owned through a VIE
structure, regulated by Chinese authorities who in 2020-2021 demonstrated willingness to dismantle entire fintech
business models overnight (Ant Group IPO, P2P lending purge). The 20-F flags China's evolving
consumer-protection rules, fee-rate compression risk, ability of regulators to revoke licensing or restrict
cross-border data flows, and PCAOB audit-access overhang. The variable-interest entity (VIE) structure means
US shareholders have contractual — not equity — claims on the onshore operating businesses; PRC courts have
never definitively enforced VIE contracts. ADR holders also face delisting risk under the HFCAA. Service fee
rates "may decline in the future" — management's own language. The operating-segment data shows large
eliminations and intercompany flows that obscure clean unit economics. Geographic diversification is real but
tiny relative to mainland China exposure.
The bull case is purely valuation-driven. At P/B 0.37 and a low single-digit P/E, you are paying $0.37 for $1 of
book and roughly one year of run-rate earnings. Management has a stated dividend policy of paying out at least
15% of prior-year net income on a consolidated basis, with a US$0.10/share cash dividend paid in July 2023.
whatScreenCantSee:
The 20-F discloses RMB-denominated financials that swing materially between segment cuts. Cash and cash
equivalents at the consolidated total were RMB 540.5 million — but most of that sits inside mainland China VIE
entities, not at the Cayman parent, meaning ADR holders don't have unrestricted access. The dividend policy of
"15% of NI" is a board-determined floor, not a binding obligation; the actual 2023 cash dividend was modest.
Walking-around money for borrowers includes Indonesia/Mexico sub-RMB-50M loans to subsidiaries —
early-stage international optionality but not material. The accounts receivable and contract assets line of RMB
2.99 billion is the working-capital lifeblood — quality of those receivables in a China consumer-credit downturn
is a key swing factor. A profitable platform earning low-single-digit times-earnings is either a screaming buy or a
value trap; the VIE structure tilts toward the latter for most fiduciary investors.

name: EverQuote, Inc.
analystSummary:
EverQuote is the leading online marketplace connecting auto-insurance shoppers with carriers and agents,
monetizing per-referral via a real-time auction model where carriers bid for high-intent consumer leads. The 2025
10-K shows the recovery from the 2022-23 carrier-pullback shock is now fully in motion: revenue of $692.5
million for 2025, up 38.5% from $500.2 million in 2024, with the automotive vertical accounting for $183.7
million of the $192.3 million revenue increase as carriers reopened marketing budgets. Net income flipped to
$99.3 million in 2025 versus $32.2 million in 2024 and a $51.3 million net loss in 2023. Adjusted EBITDA of
$94.6 million ($58.2M in 2024, $0.5M in 2023) confirms the operating leverage. The board authorized a share
repurchase program in July 2025; cash position is healthy with no long-term debt of consequence.
The bear case is two-pronged. First, customer concentration is severe and worsening: the largest customer was
38% of total revenue in 2025 and the second-largest 11%, meaning a single carrier withdrawal could erase ~40%
of the top line — exactly what happened in 2022 when Allstate, Progressive, and others abruptly cut acquisition
spend during the auto-insurance underwriting crisis. EverQuote exited its health-insurance vertical in 2023, so the
business is now functionally a single-vertical (auto P&C;) lead-gen platform with limited diversification beyond
home/renters as a small adjacency. Second, this is fundamentally a cyclical business tied to the auto-insurance
underwriting cycle: when combined ratios spike, carriers slash acquisition spending and EverQuote's revenue
craters with little of its own causation. Today's 38% growth is the upcycle; the next downturn looks much like
2022-23.
The bull case is that 2025 demonstrates the EverQuote business model still works structurally. The auto-insurance
industry is shifting acquisition dollars from offline (TV, direct-mail) to online lead-gen at a secular rate, and
EverQuote is positioned as the largest pure-play marketplace. Operating leverage is dramatic — 2025 adjusted
EBITDA margin of 13.7% versus 11.6% in 2024 versus 0.1% in 2023 — and there's room to expand further as
variable marketing dollars scale faster than fixed costs. The Class B controlling stake held by Link Ventures
whatScreenCantSee:
The "VMD/VMM" non-GAAP metric (variable marketing dollars and margin) is the single most important
operating KPI — it isolates revenue net of paid-media costs and tells you the unit economics of each acquired
consumer. The 2023 carrier-pullback was driven by industry-wide auto loss ratios crossing 100%, not by
EverQuote's competitive position; carriers are now writing profitably again and reopening online channels. The
S-3 shelf registration permits up to $150 million of further capital-markets issuance, which could overhang the
equity but also gives flexibility. Health-insurance vertical exit in 2023 was painful but de-risked the business from
CMS ACA marketplace volatility. The dual-class structure means Link Ventures retains voting control even with
low economic ownership — relevant if/when activists eye the cash on the balance sheet.

name: Daily Journal Corporation
analystSummary:
Daily Journal is a unique two-headed beast: a small legal/business publishing operation (the Traditional Business
— 	Daily 	Journals 	in 	CA/AZ, 	judicial 	profiles, 	court 	rules 	services) 	plus 	Journal 	Technologies, 	a
SaaS-and-services court case-management software business that now produces approximately 80% of
consolidated revenue (vs 76% in fiscal 2024). Sitting on top is the legendary investment portfolio originally
assembled and managed by Charlie Munger until his death on November 28, 2023. As of September 30, 2025 the
marketable-securities portfolio had a market value of approximately $493.0 million on an adjusted cost basis of
approximately $139.1 million — a cumulative unrealized gain of $353.9 million before estimated taxes of $91.4
million. The portfolio remains concentrated in just six companies, generated $7.4 million in dividends and interest
in fiscal 2025, and total assets stood at $548.1 million.
The bear case is that the Munger magic is genuinely irreplaceable, and the company says so explicitly. The 10-K
states the board "expects" the portfolio to be managed and harvested primarily to fund Journal Technologies
development going forward — which is the language of a company that no longer believes the
marketable-securities arm will compound at superior returns. The operating businesses are mediocre on their own:
Journal Technologies is a perpetually-money-losing-to-breakeven public-sector SaaS implementation business
with long sales cycles, professional-services dependence, lumpy lawsuits and competitive bidding processes. The
Traditional Business is a melting ice cube — print legal newspapers, public-notice advertising regulation under
attack. Six-stock concentration risk is enormous; a downturn in any single major holding will whip net income
and book value violently. Steven Myhill-Jones is CEO and Chairman per the FY25 signature page — leadership
transition still being digested.
The bull case rests on the gap between book value (where the portfolio is marked) and intrinsic value of Journal
Technologies if it can finally scale. P/B 1.95 understates the situation: the marketable securities are at fair-value
mark-to-market, but Journal Technologies — a sticky, switching-cost-heavy public-sector SaaS franchise serving
whatScreenCantSee:
The "P/E 8.0" is essentially meaningless because reported earnings are dominated by unrealized gains on
marketable securities — fiscal 2025 saw $134.3 million of pretax unrealized gains versus only $4.2 million from
operating income. Strip out the portfolio mark-to-market and Journal Technologies + Traditional are roughly
breakeven. Deferred tax liability on unrealized gains is approximately $91.4 million, so true after-tax net asset
value attributable to the portfolio is closer to $400 million than $493 million. The portfolio uses margin
borrowing collateralized by securities to fund Journal Technologies acquisitions and working capital — a Munger
trick — and is therefore subject to margin calls in a sharp drawdown. The company has paid no dividends in fiscal
2025 or 2024 and has no current plans to. Six-stock concentration: per published 13-Fs, the portfolio still tilts
heavily toward US bank holdings.

name: Perfect Corp.
analystSummary:
Perfect Corp. (NYSE: PERF) builds AI- and AR-powered virtual try-on, skin-analysis and image-generation tools
sold to beauty/fashion brands (B2B) and to consumers via the YouCam app suite (B2C subscriptions). FY25
revenue was $69.2M (+14.9% YoY), driven by $61.1M of "AI- and AR- cloud solutions and subscription"
revenue (+13.5%). Net income was $4.6M; operating cash flow was $13.3M. The screen flag — net cash
$161.6M against market cap $177M (91% ratio) — is grounded in $126.0M cash and equivalents plus $36.3M
six-month time deposits and $10.2M U.S. Treasuries (per the Q4 release). 908,000 active YouCam subscribers
and 859 brand clients (135 "Key Customers" generating >$50K trailing twelve months). Spun out of Taiwan
Stock Exchange-listed CyberLink, which remains a major shareholder and lessor.
The bear case begins with the dual-class structure: per the 20-F, "each holder of Class B Ordinary Shares is
entitled to ten (10) votes per share" and CEO Chang holds 100% of Class B = 66.6% voting power. That control is
now being weaponized via the take-private offer at $1.95 — a price below the prior trading range and at a
discount to net cash. Filings flag that the dual-class structure may make Class A "ineligible for inclusion in any of
these indices." Operating loss of $1.7M for FY25 reflects the company's reliance on financial income and a $2.0M
goodwill impairment from January 2025's Wannaby acquisition. App-store dependency (Apple/Google) is
explicit. Key Customer count just fell from 142 to 135 in a single quarter, hinting at B2B churn even as B2C
subscriber count declined 4% sequentially.
whatScreenCantSee:
Three filing-only items. (1) Related-party rents and the "Perfect CyberLink Education Foundation" tie the
operating company tightly to CyberLink — an item that becomes very relevant in a take-private negotiation, since
CyberLink is now both bidder and landlord. (2) Geographic disclosure: "revenue generated from the PRC
accounted for less than 3% of our total revenue" — China exposure is small, removing one common
emerging-tech overhang. (3) The Wannaby acquisition (January 2025, Lithuania-based) added headcount and
R&D; burn and was promptly written down; this is the kind of value-destructive M&A; that makes a low-ball
take-private easier to justify. Insider activity to watch: Chang, Wei-Hsin Tsen and Weichuan Liu collectively own
minor Class A stakes — but Chang's full Class B block (16.79M shares = 16.5% economic, 66.6% voting) drives
the deal math.

name: RADCOM Ltd.
analystSummary:
RADCOM is an Israel-headquartered software company that sells "service assurance" — the systems that monitor
whether AT&T;'s, Rakuten Mobile's and Verizon's mobile networks are actually delivering performance to
subscribers. The screen flagged it because cash and short-term deposits of ~$109.9M sit against a ~$218M market
cap (49% of mcap), the company is profitable (TTM net income ~$12M), and it just printed a record FY2025 with
$71.5M revenue (+17.2%). Gross margin reached ~76.8%. Six consecutive years of growth. 2026 guidance:
8–12% revenue growth (~$78.6M midpoint). It's a small-cap software company priced like a melting ice cube but
acting like a steady compounder.
The bear case starts with concentration: ~86% of 2025 revenue came from the top three customers (AT&T; and
Rakuten Mobile are public; Verizon and Vodafone have been historical names). Lose one, lose roughly a quarter
to a third of revenue overnight — and these are deals that ramp slowly, so the offsetting wins take 18–36 months.
Lumpy bookings make any single year of guidance vulnerable to a slipped contract. RADCOM is also
Israel-based: geopolitical disruption, currency (shekel) exposure, and a thin float (~$220M mcap, modest daily
volume) all amplify volatility. Slowing growth guidance (8–12% vs. 17% delivered) suggests management sees a
deceleration into 2026 absent a new Tier-1 win.
whatScreenCantSee:
The 86% top-three concentration looks scary in a screen — but the same filings reveal these are multi-year,
deeply embedded software contracts inside Tier-1 5G core deployments, not transactional sales. AT&T; has been
a customer for years; Rakuten Mobile chose RADCOM as its assurance vendor at the beginning of its greenfield
mobile build and stayed as it crossed 10M subscribers in December 2025. Switching costs in network assurance
are extreme: the software ingests live network telemetry across thousands of nodes, and rip-and-replace creates
blind spots no carrier accepts mid-rollout. The screen also can't see the operating margin trajectory — record
margins in 2025 imply most incremental revenue is dropping to the bottom line, which compounds quickly on a
sub-$80M revenue base.

name: monday.com Ltd.
analystSummary:
monday.com sells a horizontal "Work OS" — visual workflows, dashboards, CRM, dev, service modules — to
~250K+ paid customers globally. FY2025 revenue $1.232B (+27%), Q4 $334M (+25%). Customers >$50K
ARR: 4,281 (+34% YoY); >$100K ARR: 1,756 (+45%); >$500K ARR: 87 (+74%). NDR was 116% in both
>$50K and >$100K cohorts. Adjusted FCF $322.7M (26% margin). 14% non-GAAP operating margin in 2025.
Cash and equivalents ~$1.5B with no debt. The screen flagged it for cash >40% of mcap; the 2026 guide is
$1.452-$1.462B revenue (18-19% growth) and $275-$290M adjusted FCF. The Q4 print + soft guide drove the
de-rating that made this name screen-eligible at all.
The bear case is competitive intensity and growth deceleration. Asana, Atlassian, ClickUp, Smartsheet, Notion
and Microsoft Loop all overlap — and AI-native upstarts threaten the entire workflow-builder category.
Management already embedded weak no-touch SMB channel performance into 2026 guidance, plus 100-200bp
FX headwinds from shekel appreciation (55% of staff in Israel). 18-19% growth from a 27% base is a real
slowdown. Israel HQ creates geopolitical, FX, and talent risks. Multiple compression continued through 2025 as
the market re-priced unprofitable-to-marginally-profitable SaaS. SBC remains material (typical SaaS dilution
dynamics) — the "cash fortress" optic understates dilution if shares grow ~3-4%/yr.
whatScreenCantSee:
The screen captures "cash 44% of mcap" but misses that nearly all of that cash is operating cash flow generated,
not raised — meaning MNDY has crossed the threshold most "Rule of 40" SaaS companies never reach: it is
self-funding and accreting cash at >$300M/yr. The filings also show enterprise momentum (87 customers
>$500K ARR, +74%) that mid-market software companies rarely achieve without acquisition. And monday.com
runs a multi-product strategy (Work OS + CRM + Dev + Service + Vibe) where each module is a separate
land-and-expand vector — the screen gives no credit for cross-sell optionality. Finally, the Israeli
founder-operator model (similar to Wix, Fiverr, Check Point) historically outperforms US-HQ SaaS on capital
efficiency.

name: Curbline Properties Corp.
analystSummary:
Curbline owns and acquires "convenience" strip centers — small (typically <50,000 sq ft) unanchored or
shadow-anchored centers on high-traffic corridors, leased to service tenants (quick-serve restaurants, salons,
medical, financial services). At spin it owned 79 properties, almost entirely in wealthy Sun Belt and coastal
suburban submarkets contributed by SITC. The company was launched with roughly $500M of net cash and an
thesis:
First pure-play 'convenience real estate' REIT spun from SITE Centers with ~$500M net cash and a long runway
to deploy into a fragmented ~$70k-property niche.
WHY MISPRICED
Optically expensive at ~23x AFFO until you adjust for the massive net cash position used to fund accretive
acquisitions; spin from a struggling shopping-center REIT (SITC) means most legacy holders dumped without
ever assessing the new vehicle. Limited sell-side coverage and small float keep it off most institutional screens.
EDGE
CURB is acquiring convenience centers at high-cap-rate spreads to its cost of equity and sits on dry powder
representing roughly 17% of its market cap; AFFO/share growth runway should re-rate the multiple as cash gets
put to work and net debt-funded growth amplifies returns. Owns just 78 of ~68,678 US convenience properties —
TAM is enormous.
whatScreenCantSee:
Form 10 details that matter: (1) the spin was structured as a tax-free distribution AFTER SITC sold roughly $1.7B
of "non-Curbline" assets and pushed the proceeds into CURB as cash — meaning CURB launched debt-free with
the proceeds of SITC's 2023-24 disposition program, an unusual and very founder-friendly capitalization. (2)
Tenant base in the contributed portfolio is heavily service-oriented (food service, beauty, medical, financial), with
the top-10 tenants under 20% of ABR — far less concentration than typical strip-center REITs. (3) There is a
transition services agreement with SITC for accounting and IT (limited duration, ~12 months) but no long-term
operational dependency — CURB took the operating bench with it. (4) Management compensation is heavily
skewed to AFFO-per-share growth and TSR, aligning with the deployment thesis.
catalyst:
• Quarterly acquisition pace updates
• First dividend increases
• Inclusion in REIT indices once seasoned
• Equity raises if deployment accelerates

name: South Bow Corporation
analystSummary:
South Bow operates the Keystone Pipeline System — the 4,900km crude oil pipeline running from Hardisty,
Alberta to Cushing, Oklahoma and the US Gulf Coast — plus the Marketlink system (Cushing to Houston/Port
Arthur) and the Houston terminal complex. Importantly, the spin assets exclude Keystone XL (the cancelled
thesis:
Pure-play Keystone liquids pipeline spun from TC Energy with ~94% contracted cash flows and a ~7% dividend
yield — orphan stock for both Canadian dividend funds and US energy MLP benchmarks.
WHY MISPRICED
Canadian-domiciled spin trades on NYSE without index inclusion, creating dual orphan-status; many TRP
holders sold on receipt because they wanted natural-gas/regulated-utility exposure not crude pipelines. Keystone
segment carries lingering safety/litigation overhang from prior leaks that obscures the contracted earnings stream.
EDGE
Market underweights how heavily contracted (~94% recurring) revenues are post-Keystone XL settlement;
Canadian heavy-crude supply growth into Gulf Coast refiners is structurally underserved with very few
competing pipes. As an obscure spin, every quarter of clean operations should compress the discount to FCF peers
like Plains All American.
whatScreenCantSee:
Form 10 details: (1) the inter-co debt allocation — TRP loaded SOBO with roughly USD $4.8B of debt at
separation, sized to maintain investment-grade ratings (BBB area) and to send cash back to the parent; this is a
classic "parent dumps debt on the cleaner asset" mechanic but the cash flow comfortably services it. (2) Transition
Services Agreements with TRP cover IT, treasury, and certain accounting functions for up to 24 months — South
Bow is building these in-house but the headline G&A; run-rate will normalize higher in 2025-26. (3) Cross-border
tax structuring: SOBO is a Canadian corporation paying its dividend in USD with a 15% Canadian withholding
tax for US holders (recoverable in taxable accounts via foreign tax credit, lost in IRAs unless held below the
IRA-treaty threshold). This nuance alone keeps many US retail income investors away. (4) The Keystone XL
cancellation suit settlement and remaining environmental remediation costs are quantified in the risk factors at
modest levels.
catalyst:
• Quarterly contracted-volume updates
• Potential Blackrod or Hardisty terminal expansions
• Settlement of remaining historical leak liabilities
• Re-rating as MLP-style yield investors discover it

name: Everus Construction Group, Inc.
analystSummary:
Everus Construction Group operates two segments: Electrical & Mechanical (E&M;) — commercial,
institutional, industrial, and data-center construction services — and Transmission & Distribution (T&D;) —
overhead/underground utility line work and substations. MDU built the business via 30+ years of bolt-on
acquisitions; revenue at spin was approximately $3.0B with mid-single-digit EBITDA margins and a backlog
thesis:
Specialty electrical/mechanical contractor spun from MDU at a backlog levered to the data-center &
grid-modernization capex super-cycle.
WHY MISPRICED
MDU is held by utility/dividend funds that immediately sold the cyclical construction stub; sell-side coverage was
sparse at spin. Original spin valuation models assumed peer multiples of ~14x EBITDA, but the data-center mix
has accelerated faster than expected.
EDGE
Backlog has grown to $2.8B with mid-single-digit EBITDA margin expansion as data-center work mix rises;
ECG is becoming a pure-play 'pick-and-shovel' on hyperscaler buildouts that the market is still pricing as a
regional electrical contractor. Insider buying and modest analyst initiation underway.
whatScreenCantSee:
Form 10 details: (1) Capital structure — ECG launched with essentially no parent-allocated long-term debt and
just a working-capital revolver; this is unusual for a spin and reflects MDU keeping its utility leverage. The clean
balance sheet plus ~$200M+ of cash means ECG has dry powder for tuck-in acquisitions in a fragmented
contractor universe. (2) Customer concentration disclosed in the Form 10 shows no single customer above ~10%
of revenue, but the top-10 includes hyperscalers and major utility customers — meaning data-center mix is real
and contracted, not just project-by-project bidding. (3) Transition Services Agreement with MDU is short-dated
(~12-18 months) for IT, payroll, treasury — minimal long-term parent dependency. (4) The Form 10 explicitly
references "sponsor M&A;" as a use of capital and management compensation is heavily weighted toward
EBITDA growth and TSR — strong alignment with the deployment thesis.
catalyst:
• Quarterly backlog disclosures showing data-center mix
• Margin expansion vs. peers MTZ/PRIM
• Potential M&A; using clean balance sheet
• Index inclusion once 12-month seasoning passes

name: Magnera Corporation
thesis:
World's largest nonwovens producer formed by spinning Berry's HHNF business and merging with Glatfelter —
micro-cap orphan trading at <3x EBITDA with a self-help margin story.
WHY MISPRICED
Berry shareholders received only ~10% of their value in Magnera shares and sold indiscriminately given
micro-cap size and low yield. Heavy debt (~$1.7B net) on $370M EBITDA scares quality investors, and
Glatfelter's prior operational issues leave a stigma. No sell-side coverage from the major banks.
EDGE
Pro-forma synergies from the Berry/Glatfelter combination are only ~30% realized; merger cost-out plus working
capital release should drive FCF inflection in 2026 that the market hasn't underwritten. EV/EBITDA <6x vs.
specialty packaging peers at 9-11x; even modest deleveraging double-digit equity re-rate is plausible.
whatScreenCantSee:
Form 10/S-4 details: (1) Capital structure — Berry pushed approximately $1.5-1.7B of debt down into MAGN at
separation (the classic "RMT debt dump") in the form of senior secured notes and term loans; the cash transfer
back to Berry was the entire economic point of the structure for BERY shareholders and explains the leverage. (2)
Customer contracts with the hygiene majors are typically 2-3 year MSAs with quarterly volume true-ups; pricing
typically passes through resin (polypropylene) costs with a 1-quarter lag, which protects margins on the way up
but can squeeze on the way down. (3) Transition Services Agreement with Berry covers shared services for up to
24 months — G&A; run-rate will normalize higher in 2025-26 as MAGN stands up standalone systems. (4)
Glatfelter's prior environmental remediation liabilities (legacy paper mills) transferred into MAGN — disclosed
in risk factors at modest amounts but worth monitoring.
catalyst:
• Quarterly synergy realization updates
• Refinancing of post-spin debt at lower coupon
• Divestiture of non-core mills
• First analyst initiations from packaging coverage teams

name: Concentra Group Holdings Parent, Inc.
analystSummary:
Concentra operates the largest network of occupational-health centers in the US — 549 medical centers across 41
states plus 156 onsite clinics located inside employer facilities (manufacturing plants, distribution centers,
corporate campuses). The business is dominated by two recurring revenue streams: workers'-compensation injury
thesis:
#1 US occupational-health platform (549 centers + 156 onsite clinics) spun from Select Medical with a dominant
share in employer-paid workers'-comp clinics.
WHY MISPRICED
SEM was held primarily by inpatient-rehab investors who sold the outpatient occ-health stub; IPO/spin
combination structure (June IPO then November distribution) caused two waves of forced selling. Boring B2B
niche means little retail interest and limited bulge-bracket coverage relative to size.
EDGE
DOT physical and workers'-comp exam mandates create utility-like recurring volume immune to economic
cycles; Concentra has pricing power and consolidation runway. ~$1.8B revenue with mid-teens margins yet
trades at high-single-digit EBITDA multiple vs healthcare-services peers at 11-13x.
whatScreenCantSee:
Form 10/S-1 details: (1) Capital structure — Concentra raised approximately $585M in IPO proceeds in July
2024, of which roughly $523M was used to repay intercompany debt to SEM (effectively a dividend recap), then
issued ~$1.5B of senior notes and term loans pre-distribution, also largely upstreamed to SEM; this is a classic
"parent strips cash from spin" mechanic that explains why the leverage is what it is. (2) Long-term agreements
with SEM include shared services (IT, certain HR, real estate) for up to 24 months at cost-plus pricing; the
headline G&A; run-rate will normalize as Concentra stands up standalone functions. (3) Workers'-comp
regulatory framework is state-by-state — about 25 states have "employer choice" of provider (favorable to CON)
vs ~25 with "employee choice" (less favorable); this granularity is in the risk factors and matters for the M&A;
geographic strategy. (4) Top employer clients include Fortune-100 manufacturers and logistics companies;
200,000+ clients with no single client >3% — true diversification. (5) Management holds meaningful equity
post-spin and the comp plan is heavily weighted to EBITDA growth and same-center volume.
catalyst:
• Quarterly same-clinic visit growth
• Onsite-clinic enterprise wins (announced employer contracts)
• M&A; roll-up of independent occ-health clinics
• First post-spin investor day

name: Amentum Holdings, Inc.
analystSummary:
Amentum is the post-merger combination of Jacobs' Critical Mission Solutions plus parts of its Cyber &
Intelligence unit with the legacy Amentum business — yielding the second-largest pure-play federal services
